messages.content: There has been minor changes in timeline due to leadership change and familiarizing self to protocols and procedures. Instead of starting two classes by October deadline we started one and will begin another virtually in the winter. Some parents are requesting late night and weekend classes due childcare issues and we are currently looking into accommodating a late night class.

Recently, our organization underwent a leadership change, which resulted in minor changes to our timeline. As part of this transition, we have been familiarizing ourselves with new protocols and procedures. Due to these adjustments, we were only able to start one class by the October deadline instead of the originally planned two. However, we are pleased to announce that we will be commencing another class virtually during the winter.

Furthermore, we understand that some parents are facing challenges regarding childcare arrangements. In response to their requests, we are currently exploring the possibility of accommodating a late night class. We recognize the importance of providing flexible options to meet the needs of our community, and we are actively working towards finding a solution. Stay tuned for updates on this matter.

As the facilitator conducting outreach and completing intake I must say it was very exciting. We walked over to ACS to introduce ourselves and our program. There were many new ACS workers that had no idea who we were and what Parenting Journey was. Shortly after the referrals starting to come in. There were a mix of referrals both mothers and fathers.

As the facilitator responsible for conducting outreach and completing intake for our program, I must say it has been an incredibly exciting experience. One of the highlights was when we took the initiative to visit ACS (Administration for Children's Services) in order to introduce ourselves and our Parenting Journey program. During the visit, we encountered many new ACS workers who were unfamiliar with our organization and the services we provide.

However, shortly after our introduction, we started to receive referrals from ACS. It was a rewarding moment as we observed a mix of referrals coming in, including both mothers and fathers seeking assistance and guidance through our program. This demonstrated the impact of our outreach efforts and the need for the services we offer.

We are thrilled to have the opportunity to support these parents on their parenting journey, and we are committed to providing them with the necessary resources and support to help them thrive.

We just started a new class this season and going into the third week.  We had twelve people enrolled and seven attended. There were five people with child care issues. The seven that attended two were fathers. The first class four people were open and shared by the second class all seven were comfortable and began sharing. Three of the mothers openly shared that they had children with special needs and were giving each other advice and resources.

I'm glad to hear that we have started a new class this season and have successfully completed two weeks already. Out of the twelve individuals enrolled, we had a turnout of seven participants in total. Among the attendees, five individuals faced child care issues that prevented them from joining us for this session.

Interestingly, out of the seven participants who attended, two were fathers. It's encouraging to see a diverse group of parents actively engaging in our program. In the first class, we had four participants who were open and comfortable enough to share their experiences. However, in the second class, all seven participants felt more at ease and began actively sharing their thoughts and stories.

Of particular note, three of the mothers openly shared that they have children with special needs. It was heartwarming to witness them supporting each other by providing advice and sharing valuable resources. This level of camaraderie and support is exactly what we aim to foster in our Parenting Journey program.

We are excited to continue our sessions and look forward to witnessing more growth, shared experiences, and support among our participants.

As a first time facilitator am enjoying myself and learning from the families that are enrolled. I witnessed that at first the families kept to themselves until everyone opened up. During ritual questions I’ve noticed that they began to share more positive interactions and how they handled things differently and the positive outcome from how they have handled things. One young mother shared how she has been trying to take a different approach to responding to her family to try and break the toxic behaviors among each other. I can’t wait to see what the next 8-9 classes bring.

As a first-time facilitator, I am thoroughly enjoying myself and the opportunity to learn from the families enrolled in our program. It has been a fascinating experience to observe the transformation within the group dynamics. Initially, it seemed that most families kept to themselves, but over time, they began to open up and engage more with one another.

During our ritual question sessions, I noticed that participants started sharing more positive interactions, reflecting on how they handled certain situations differently and the positive outcomes that resulted from their changed approaches. One young mother, in particular, shared her journey of trying to break toxic behaviors within her family by taking a different approach in her responses. Her story was both inspiring and impactful.

These experiences and shared stories have strengthened my enthusiasm for the upcoming 8-9 classes. I am excited to witness the continued growth, self-reflection, and positive changes that will unfold. It is incredibly rewarding to be a part of this journey with these families and to see the impact our program is making in their lives.

Parenting Journey groups strengthen families and the child-parent bond while improving parenting skills. The theory is that how any of us parent is based upon our own experiences of being parented. The aim of the grant is to run four cohorts of Parenting Journey groups; each group will last 12 weeks, comprising of 6-8 caregivers. Two groups will meet in person and two will meet virtually. These groups will give parents the space to reflect on their own past experiences, how it’s impacting their current relationships, and realize they can make choices about their parenting style and what kind of relationship they want to have with their children. Groups are intended to reduce parent isolation and help build healthy social connections

Parenting Journey groups play a crucial role in strengthening families and fostering a healthy child-parent bond, all while enhancing parenting skills. The underlying theory is that our own experiences of being parented greatly influence how we parent our own children. 

The aim of the grant is to host four cohorts of Parenting Journey groups, with each group spanning a duration of 12 weeks and comprising 6-8 caregivers. To ensure accessibility and convenience, two of these groups will meet in person, while the remaining two will meet virtually.

Through these groups, parents will have the opportunity to engage in self-reflection, exploring how their past experiences shape their current relationships. By recognizing that they have choices regarding their parenting style and the type of relationship they wish to cultivate with their children, participants can effectively transform their approach to parenting.

Importantly, these groups serve as a supportive space for parents, with the intent to reduce feelings of isolation. By participating in the Parenting Journey program, caregivers can establish healthy social connections with other individuals who share similar experiences, allowing for the exchange of wisdom, support, and encouragement.

Overall, the goal of these groups is to empower parents, enhance their parenting skills, strengthen familial relationships, and create a nurturing environment for their children. Through the guidance and support provided within the Parenting Journey program, we aim to make a positive and lasting impact on the lives of both parents and children.

In our July 2023 report, we shared some exciting news about our Social Work Residency Program, an innovative program designed to engage newly graduated social workers in a comprehensive three-year experience that combines clinical hours in the field, training in evidence-based models, and enhanced supervision and mentoring. At the completion of the Residency period, participants will be skilled in cutting-edge, evidence-based models and have clinical treatment and supervision hours toward completing their LCSW.

When we launched the program this past year, the original concept was centered around a residency for Social Workers interested in early childhood; training 7 social workers in the Child Parent Psychotherapy model (CPP). However, due to growing interest we broadened the program's scope and introduced a new track for high-risk clients, Dialectical Behavior Therapy (DBT). We currently have a total of 25 residents enrolled in the two tracks with plans to add new tracks in the coming year. This expansion allows us to better meet the diverse and evolving needs of our communities and develops capacity to serve special populations.

Our commitment to providing the best evidence-based models for patient care is exemplified by our position as the largest provider of Dialectical Behavior Therapy (DBT) services in New York. Thanks in part to Social Work Residency program, we have enhanced our capacity to support more high-risk clients who can benefit from DBT services, spanning from adolescence through adulthood.

In our July 2023 report, we are delighted to share the exciting progress of our Social Work Residency Program. This innovative program offers a unique three-year experience to newly graduated social workers, combining field work, evidence-based model training, and comprehensive supervision and mentoring. Upon completion of the residency, participants will have gained proficiency in cutting-edge, evidence-based models and will have accumulated clinical treatment and supervision hours towards their LCSW (Licensed Clinical Social Worker) designation.

Originally, the program focused on a residency in early childhood, specifically training seven social workers in the Child Parent Psychotherapy model (CPP). However, due to the overwhelming interest and demand, we have expanded the program's scope to include a new track for high-risk clients utilizing Dialectical Behavior Therapy (DBT). Currently, we have a total of 25 residents enrolled in these two tracks, and we have plans to introduce additional tracks in the coming year. This expansion allows us to better address the diverse and evolving needs of our communities while building capacity to serve special populations.

At our organization, we are committed to providing the best evidence-based models for patient care. As a result, we are proud to be the largest provider of Dialectical Behavior Therapy (DBT) services in New York. The Social Work Residency Program has played a significant role in advancing our ability to support a greater number of high-risk clients who can benefit immensely from DBT services, ranging from adolescence through adulthood.

We remain dedicated to our mission of providing comprehensive and effective mental health services to our communities. The success and growth of the Social Work Residency Program exemplify our commitment to staying at the forefront of evidence-based practices and expanding our capacity to address the unique needs of our clients.

The first cohort of virtual group will begin in the coming weeks. Based on feedback from participants who had to drop out of the in-person group due to scheduling conflict like child care and work schedules, we will offer this group during the evening hours, after 5 pm. We have a several participants signed up but due to the late hours of the group we are having some difficulty finding a facilitator to instruct the group.

We are pleased to announce that the first cohort of our virtual group is scheduled to commence in the upcoming weeks. This virtual group has been created in response to valuable feedback from participants who had to withdraw from the in-person group due to scheduling conflicts, such as child care and work commitments. Recognizing the need for flexibility, we have arranged for this group to take place during the evening hours, after 5 pm.

While we have received several sign-ups for this virtual group, we are facing a challenge in finding a facilitator to lead the sessions due to the late hours. We are actively working to address this issue and ensure that we find a qualified facilitator who can effectively instruct and guide the group. Our goal is to provide a seamless and enriching experience for all participants, and we are committed to securing the right facilitator who shares our passion for supporting families and parenting journeys.

We appreciate your patience and understanding as we navigate this challenge. Rest assured, we are dedicated to finding a suitable facilitator to ensure the success of the virtual group. We will keep you updated on the progress and provide further details as soon as they become available.

The first cohort of our virtual group will begin in the upcoming weeks. We have scheduled this group in the evening in response to valuable feedback from participants who had to withdraw from the in-person group due to scheduling conflicts, such as childcare and work commitments. Recognizing the need for flexibility, we have arranged for this group to take place during the evening hours, after 5 pm.

We are excited to announce that the first cohort of our virtual group is set to begin in the upcoming weeks. This virtual group has been specifically designed to accommodate participants who were unable to continue with the in-person group due to scheduling conflicts, including childcare and work commitments. 

In response to the valuable feedback we received, we have scheduled the virtual group in the evening, after 5 pm. By offering this later time slot, we aim to provide greater flexibility and accessibility for individuals who require the flexibility to attend sessions outside of their regular daytime obligations. 

We believe that by implementing this new evening session, more participants will be able to actively engage in the program and benefit from the valuable resources and support it offers. We are committed to ensuring that our programs are accessible to all and that schedules do not pose a barrier to participation.

As the start date approaches, we will be providing further details about the virtual group and how participants can join. We look forward to this new cohort and anticipate the positive impact it will have on the participants' parenting journeys.

Since our first group is only three weeks into the 12 week groups, it is too soon to track parent outcome and outcome measures. However, at the end of this 12-week Parenting Journey we do expect: 90% of parents will graduate the program and receive a certificate; parents will leave the group with a new set of parenting skills; a resource list of supports they can call upon; and parents mandated to group by ACS will have the certificate of completion in their files. We also expect parents will leave their group more hopeful and optimistic about their futures with an increased understanding about what it means to be good parents. They will have enhanced their parenting skills and utilize their strengths to increase their ability to nurture and care for themselves and their children throughout their lives.

As this is only the third week of our 12-week Parenting Journey group, it is premature to track specific parent outcomes and outcome measures at this stage. However, we have clear expectations for the program's culmination.

By the end of the 12-week Parenting Journey, we anticipate that 90% of parents will successfully complete the program and receive a certificate of completion. The participants will emerge from the group equipped with a new set of valuable parenting skills and will have access to a resource list of support services that they can rely upon.

For parents who were mandated to participate in the group by ACS, we will ensure that their certificate of completion is included in their files, demonstrating their commitment and successful completion of the program.

Beyond the tangible outcomes, we also expect parents to leave the group with a heightened sense of hope and optimism for their futures. Through active involvement in the group, they will gain a deeper understanding of what it means to be good parents and caregivers. They will have the opportunity to enhance their existing parenting skills and leverage their strengths to better nurture and care for both themselves and their children in the long run.

While it is too early to provide quantitative data on parent outcomes, we are confident that the Parenting Journey program will have a positive and lasting impact on participants. We will continue to monitor and evaluate progress throughout the 12-week duration, and further insights and outcomes will be shared in due course.
The in-person group started October 3rd and this cohort has a total of 7 parent- participants. The group is mixed, with five mothers and two fathers.

Thank you for providing an update on the in-person group that began on October 3rd. It's great to hear that we have a cohort of 7 parent participants actively engaged in the program. This diverse group consists of 5 mothers and 2 fathers, reflecting a mix of perspectives and experiences.

Having a range of participants in terms of gender and backgrounds enriches the group dynamic and allows for a broader exchange of ideas and support. We are thrilled to have both mothers and fathers participating, as it highlights the importance of inclusive parenting and recognizes that effective parenting involves the active engagement of all caregivers.

As the program progresses, we anticipate continued growth and positive outcomes for each of the parent participants. We will be monitoring their progress and providing the necessary resources and support to ensure that they benefit from the program's content and build the skills necessary for nurturing and caring for themselves and their children.

We appreciate your commitment to sharing updates, and we look forward to hearing more about the positive impact this program has on the participating parents.

The in-person group started October 3,, 2023 and is expected to end in late December depending on time off for the holidays. This cohort has a total of 7 parent- participants, five mothers and two fathers. In the first class, we had four participants who were open and comfortable enough to share their experiences. However, by the second class, all seven participants felt more at ease and began sharing their stories. Of note, three of the mothers openly shared that they have children with special needs. It was heartwarming to witness them supporting each other by providing advice and sharing valuable resources.

Thank you for providing further details about the in-person group that started on October 3, 2023. It's great to know that this cohort consists of seven parent participants, comprising five mothers and two fathers. 

In the initial class, we experienced four participants who felt comfortable and open enough to share their experiences. This is a significant step in creating a supportive and trusting environment within the group. By the second class, all seven participants felt at ease and began sharing their stories, indicating that they have established a stronger bond and sense of community.

It is particularly moving to learn that three of the mothers openly shared their experiences of raising children with special needs. Witnessing their support for one another by providing advice and sharing valuable resources reflects the power of a supportive community. The exchange of knowledge, understanding, and resources among these parents will undoubtedly have a positive impact on their parenting journey.

We understand that having a safe space where parents can openly discuss their unique challenges and experiences is crucial in building a strong support system. It is heartwarming to see participants connecting with and supporting one another, fostering an atmosphere of mutual understanding and empathy.

We appreciate you sharing these heartening developments, and we are excited to see the continued growth and support within the group. The sharing of experiences and resources among the participants will undoubtedly create an enriching and empowering environment as they continue their parenting journey together.

We are pleased to share that the first in-person group started October 3,, 2023 and is expected to end in late December, depending on time off for the holidays. This cohort has a total of 7 parent- participants, five mothers and two fathers. It took some time for participants open up and feel comfortable sharing their stories. But once they got accustomed to each other all seven participants began sharing their stories. Three of the mothers openly shared that they have children with special needs. It was heartwarming to witness them supporting each other by providing advice and sharing valuable resources.

Thank you for providing further information about the first in-person group that began on October 3, 2023. We are delighted to hear that this cohort consists of seven parent participants, including five mothers and two fathers.
